source_display: AI as Brain Crutches for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching.
Vrijen shared advice from his edtech experience: avoid the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes.
He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly.
A cornerstone customer providing reliable revenue creates options—whether to scale, attract others, or seek investment.
Vrijen countered that social media only works long-term when done authentically—owning one's style rather than performing.
Chris revealed he's been building his edtech product for almost a year, leveraging AI despite being a former special education teacher rather than a developer.
Vrijen invited Chris to join the Future of Higher Education group (6,000 members globally, ~1,000 in NYC, ~1,000 in Boston), where Vrijen serves as NYC co-lead.
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies.
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing.

source_display: Jobs Are Getting Paid to Learn
evidence: He explained that jobs are essentially 'getting paid to learn,' but gatekeeping makes experience inaccessible.
Maximilian argued these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape, with many PM positions facing similar elimination unless they manage P&L and have product experience.
He noted that visible links between individual work and the product/service being sold are increasingly essential in 2026.

source_display: AI Agents Need Deep Orchestrator Understanding
evidence: Vrijen connected this to the bet that AI agents with deeper understanding of the orchestrating individual will perform better than generic agents.
Vrijen observed that the market for novel hiring signals is small because snake oil assessment companies have burned goodwill in the past.

source_display: SAT Flattens Humans to Two Axes
evidence: Vrijen used the example of parents obsessing over SATs because numbers are concrete and discrete, but noted this flattens humans to two axes inappropriately.
He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs. He outlined three strategic levels.

source_display: AI Stuck Is a Mental Model Problem
evidence: Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems.
Offensive and defensive prompting: Expansive generation (expand option set) vs. critical tightening (attack, break down, shore up)
Threshold rubrics: Ask the AI to create an evaluation rubric, fill it out, assign scores, and iterate output until achieving the highest level
embrace the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team—recognizing the AI is capable of more than you initially realize
He shared his metacognitive patterns for working effectively with AI: narrative before data, use yourself as a guinea pig, never accept the first answer

source_display: Plan Files as Future of Coding
evidence: He emphasized the importance of intentional prompting—documenting AI conversations in plan files that mix prose and code in stepwise fashion. He positioned this as the future of coding.
Logan Currie demonstrated deep analysis of 18 years of LinkedIn data using Claude Code... Key discoveries included: Network mismatch: Only 3,300 LinkedIn connections versus 45,000 imported contacts

source_display: Inbound/Outbound Ratio as Social Capital Proxy
evidence: Social capital proxy: Inbound versus outbound connection ratio as a measure of professional standing
An AI productivity workshop presented by Careerspan (Vrijen Attawar and Logan Currie) and Zo Computer (Ben Guo).
Ben Guo (co-founder of Zo Computer and Vrijen as a power Zo user)
